metadata.contributionModel: Public GitHub pull requests against a single curriculum JSON

tracks.title: Computer Science Foundations
tracks.tagline: Open-source mastery learning for computational thinking.
tracks.description: An initial open computer science curriculum with a prerequisite graph, lesson checks, reviews, and local mastery state.

courses.title: Computational Thinking Core
courses.tagline: From program flow to conditions, loops, and debugging.
courses.description: This sample course shows how open content can later be extended by the community along a shared skill graph.

courses.title: CS1: Introduction to Programming
courses.tagline: Data types, functions, lists, and your first algorithms.
courses.description: A first programming course that builds from values and operators up through functions, lists, and algorithmic thinking. All examples use language-neutral pseudocode.

modules.title: Program State
modules.summary: Learn how programs change state step by step.

modules.title: Control Flow
modules.summary: Understand how conditions and loops control program flow.

modules.title: Debugging Mindset
modules.summary: Build a reproducible debugging process instead of guessing.

modules.title: Data Types and Values
modules.summary: Understand how programs represent numbers, text, and logical truth values.

modules.title: Functions and Abstraction
modules.summary: Package repeated logic into named, reusable blocks with clear inputs and outputs.

modules.title: Lists and Iteration
modules.summary: Store multiple values together and process them systematically with loops.

modules.title: Algorithms and Problem Solving
modules.summary: Express solutions as precise sequences of steps and analyze simple search strategies.

skills.title: Read sequential program flow
skills.description: Can trace a linear program flow step by step.

skills.title: Track variable state
skills.description: Can determine the current value of a variable after multiple assignments.

skills.title: Evaluate boolean branches
skills.description: Can predict which branch of an if/else decision will execute.

skills.title: Trace simple loops
skills.description: Can determine loop iterations and final state in simple examples.

skills.title: Isolate bugs with hypotheses
skills.description: Can approach a bug with reproduce-isolate-inspect instead of guessing.

skills.title: Identify basic data types
skills.description: Can recognize and distinguish integers, floats, strings, and booleans in a program.

skills.title: Apply arithmetic operators
skills.description: Can evaluate expressions using addition, subtraction, multiplication, division, and remainder on numeric values.

skills.title: Define and call functions
skills.description: Can write a named block of code and invoke it from other parts of the program.

skills.title: Use parameters and return values
skills.description: Can write functions that accept input via parameters and send a result back via a return value.

skills.title: Create and index lists
skills.description: Can create an ordered collection of values and retrieve individual elements by their zero-based index.

skills.title: Iterate over lists
skills.description: Can use a loop to visit each element in a list and apply an operation to it.

skills.title: Describe algorithm steps precisely
skills.description: Can express a computational solution as a finite, ordered sequence of unambiguous steps.

skills.title: Apply linear search
skills.description: Can trace and implement a sequential scan through a list to find a target value.

lessons.title: Read sequential programs
lessons.summary: Programs do not run magically. They execute in a precisely defined order.
lessons.objective: After this lesson, you will be able to read a linear flow and separate input, processing, and output.

sections.title: Programs are executable instructions
body: A computer does not interpret intent. It executes exactly the instructions written in the program.
When reading code, a simple lens helps: what comes in, what changes, and what comes out at the end?
sections.checkpoint: Why can swapping just two lines completely change the output?

workedExamples.prompt: read input -> double it -> output result

lessons.title: Track variable state
lessons.summary: Variables store state. Strong programmers read assignments as state transformations.
lessons.objective: You can determine the final value of a variable after multiple updates.

sections.title: Assignment changes state
body: In `score = score + 1`, you are not doing algebra. You are transforming state.
Read every assignment as: retrieve the old value, perform an operation, store the new value.
sections.checkpoint: Which two pieces of information do you need to predict the final variable value?

lessons.title: Boolean branches
lessons.summary: Conditions split program flow into possible paths.
lessons.objective: You can predict which branch of an if/else structure will execute.

sections.title: Conditions are decisions
body: A condition evaluates an expression to `true` or `false`.
After that, exactly the branch whose condition is satisfied continues running.
sections.checkpoint: Why are conditions the basis of reactive program behavior?

workedExamples.prompt: if temperature > 30 then warm else cool
steps: Determine the true or false branch.

lessons.title: Trace loops
lessons.summary: Loops compress repetition. The key skill is simulating iterations carefully.
lessons.objective: You can simulate simple loops by hand and determine the final state.

sections.title: Loops are structured repetition
body: Loops replace copy-paste with repeated execution under clear rules.
When tracing, it helps to write down the counter and every changed variable for each iteration.
sections.checkpoint: What should you write down for each iteration so you do not lose track of the loop?

workedExamples.prompt: sum = 0; for i in [1,2,3]: sum = sum + i

lessons.title: Debugging with hypotheses
lessons.summary: Good debugging is structured investigation, not frantic rewriting.
lessons.objective: You can approach a bug systematically with reproduce, isolate, inspect, and verify.

sections.title: From guesses to testable steps
body: Instead of changing code at random, form a hypothesis about the cause of the bug.
Then reduce uncertainty: reproduce, isolate, inspect state, fix, verify.
sections.checkpoint: Why is isolating a bug often more effective than immediately rewriting code?

workedExamples.prompt: A counter ends at 1 instead of 3.
steps: Log the state after each change.
Narrow the mismatch down to a specific line.

lessons.title: Data types in programs
lessons.summary: Every value in a program belongs to a type that shapes what you can do with it.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can identify integers, floats, strings, and booleans and explain why the type of a value matters.

sections.title: Values have types
body: A program works with values: numbers, text, truth values. Every value belongs to a type.

The type determines which operations make sense. Adding two integers gives a predictable numeric result; combining a number with a piece of text has no self-evident meaning and causes an error in most languages.
sections.checkpoint: Why would mixing incompatible types cause a problem in a program?

sections.title: The four primitive types
body: Integer: a whole number without a decimal point. Examples: 0, 5, -12.
Float: a number with a decimal point used to represent fractional values. Examples: 3.14, -0.5.
String: a sequence of characters enclosed in quotes, used to represent text. Examples: "hello", "42".
Boolean: a value that is either true or false, used to represent a logical state.

steps: 7 has no decimal point and is a whole number → integer.
"open" is text enclosed in quotes → string.
false is a logical value with no quotes → boolean.

lessons.title: Arithmetic operators
lessons.summary: Operators combine numeric values into new values through computation.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can evaluate arithmetic expressions step by step and predict the result, including operator precedence and the remainder operator.

sections.title: Operators transform values
body: An arithmetic operator takes one or more values and produces a new value.
The five basic operators are: + (addition), - (subtraction), * (multiplication), / (division), % (remainder after integer division).
Operator precedence: multiplication, division, and remainder are evaluated before addition and subtraction. Parentheses override precedence.
sections.checkpoint: What is the result of 3 + 4 * 2? Explain the order of evaluation.

sections.title: The remainder operator
body: The remainder operator (%) gives the leftover after an integer division. For example, 7 % 3 equals 1 because 7 = 2 * 3 + 1.
Remainder is useful for detecting whether a number is even or odd: if n % 2 equals 0 then n is even, otherwise it is odd.

steps: Left to right from that point: 2 + 12 = 14.

workedExamples.prompt: Is 18 divisible by 4? Use the remainder operator.
steps: Because the remainder is not 0, 18 is not divisible by 4.

lessons.title: Defining and calling functions
lessons.summary: Functions let you name a piece of logic so it can be reused without rewriting it.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can write a simple function definition and call it correctly.

sections.title: Why functions exist
body: Without functions, repeated logic must be written out every time. That leads to duplication, which makes programs harder to fix and understand.
A function gives a name to a reusable piece of logic. Calling the function by name runs that logic at any point in the program.
sections.checkpoint: What problem does introducing a function solve?

sections.title: Anatomy of a function
body: A function definition has three parts: a name, a body containing the instructions to execute, and an optional list of parameters.
The name identifies the function so it can be called. The body is the logic that runs when the function is called.
A call executes the function body at that point in the program.

workedExamples.prompt: Define a function named greet with no parameters that outputs the text "Hello".
steps: Inside the function body write: OUTPUT "Hello"

lessons.title: Parameters and return values
lessons.summary: Parameters let a function accept input; a return value lets it send a result back to the caller.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can write a function with parameters and a return value and call it with arguments.

sections.title: Parameters are named inputs
body: A parameter is a named placeholder inside the function definition. When the function is called, the caller supplies an actual value called an argument.
Inside the function body the parameter behaves like a local variable holding the argument value.
sections.checkpoint: What is the difference between a parameter and an argument?

sections.title: Return values send results back
body: A return statement ends the function and sends a value back to the point where the function was called.
The returned value can be stored in a variable or used directly in an expression.
A function that has no return statement produces no usable result.

workedExamples.prompt: Write a function double(n) that returns n multiplied by 2. Then call double(5).
steps: Inside the function n = 5, so n * 2 = 10.

lessons.title: Lists and indexing
lessons.summary: A list stores an ordered sequence of values that can be accessed and changed by position.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can create a list, access elements by their index, and update a specific element.

sections.title: Lists store ordered sequences
body: A list holds multiple values under a single name. Each value occupies a position called an index.
Indexes start at 0, so the first element is at index 0, the second at index 1, and so on.
Accessing an element by its index returns the value at that position without changing the list.
sections.checkpoint: A list has 5 elements. What is the index of the last element?

sections.title: Updating elements
body: An element can be replaced by assigning a new value to its index position. This changes only that element; all others remain unchanged.
The length of a list is the count of elements it currently holds.

workedExamples.prompt: scores = [10, 20, 30]. What is scores[1]? Then set scores[0] = 15. What is the list now?
steps: scores[1] accesses index 1 → the value is 20.
scores[0] = 15 replaces the element at index 0.

lessons.title: Iterating over lists
lessons.summary: A for-each loop visits every element in a list in order, making bulk processing concise and safe.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can write a loop that processes each element of a list and accumulate a result.

sections.title: For-each loops visit every element
body: A for-each loop sets a loop variable to each element in the list one at a time and runs the body once per element.
This removes the need to manage an index counter manually and prevents off-by-one errors.
sections.checkpoint: How many times does the loop body execute for a list with 4 elements?

sections.title: Accumulating a result
body: A common pattern is to start a variable at a neutral value, such as 0 for a sum or 1 for a product, and update it inside the loop.
After the loop ends, the accumulator variable holds the combined result across all elements.

workedExamples.prompt: values = [3, 7, 2]. Compute the sum using a loop.

lessons.title: Describing algorithm steps
lessons.summary: An algorithm is a finite, ordered set of instructions that solves a specific problem for any valid input.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can describe a simple algorithm as numbered steps that another person could follow without ambiguity.

sections.title: What makes a set of steps an algorithm?
body: An algorithm has three essential properties: it is finite (it eventually stops), it is precise (every step is unambiguous), and it is effective (each step can actually be carried out).
A vague instruction such as 'figure out the answer' is not a valid algorithm step because it is not precise.
sections.checkpoint: Which property ensures that an algorithm will always terminate?

sections.title: Writing algorithm steps
body: Good algorithm steps use specific action words: SET, READ, COMPUTE, COMPARE, OUTPUT.
Each step should accomplish exactly one action. If a step needs a decision, it becomes a conditional step.
Testing an algorithm on a small example is the fastest way to discover missing or incorrect steps.

workedExamples.prompt: Write an algorithm to find the larger of two numbers a and b.
steps: Step 2: IF a > b THEN SET larger = a ELSE SET larger = b.
Trace with a = 5, b = 9: 9 > 5 is false so larger = 9. Output: 9.

lessons.title: Linear search
lessons.summary: Linear search checks each element of a list one by one until the target is found or the list is exhausted.
lessons.objective: After this lesson you can trace linear search on a given list and state its best-case and worst-case behavior.

sections.title: The idea behind linear search
body: Linear search works on any list regardless of order. It begins at index 0 and compares each element to the target.
If the current element matches the target, the search succeeds and returns the index. If the entire list is exhausted without a match, the search reports failure.
sections.checkpoint: In what situation does linear search examine every element of the list?

sections.title: Best and worst cases
body: Best case: the target is the first element. Only one comparison is needed.
Worst case: the target is the last element or not present at all. Every element must be compared.
On average, linear search examines about half the list. For large lists this can be slow, which motivates more efficient search strategies.

assessments.title: Entry diagnostic
assessments.description: Determines which core skills already exist and which ones should be practiced first.

items.prompt: Which statement best describes sequential program flow?
options: The computer randomly picks a suitable line
The computer executes instructions in a defined order
The computer only executes the last line
items.correctAnswer: The computer executes instructions in a defined order
items.explanation: A sequential program follows a fixed sequence of steps.

items.prompt: `x = 2`; then `x = x + 5`. What is the final value of `x`?
items.explanation: The old value 2 is read, 5 is added, and the result is stored again.

items.prompt: If `score = 12` and the condition is `score > 10`, which branch runs?
items.explanation: 12 is greater than 10.

items.prompt: `sum = 0`; then a loop adds `1`, `2`, and `3`. What is the final value of `sum`?
What is the best first step in debugging?
options: Reproduce the bug and inspect state
items.correctAnswer: Reproduce the bug and inspect state
items.explanation: Without a reproducible bug, there is no solid basis for targeted analysis.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Read sequential programs
assessments.description: Checks whether a linear flow can be read step by step.

items.prompt: Which step comes first in a simple program flow?
items.explanation: Without input or an initial state, no processing can happen yet.

items.prompt: Put the steps of a simple flow in order.
items.explanation: The standard pattern is input, processing, output.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Track variable state
assessments.description: Checks whether assignments can be read as state changes.

items.prompt: `coins = 4`; then `coins = coins + 2`. What is the final value?
items.explanation: 4 is read, 2 is added, and the result is stored.

items.prompt: Which statement correctly describes `score = score + 1`?
items.explanation: The existing value is read, changed, and stored again.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Boolean branches
assessments.description: Checks whether conditions lead to the correct branch.

items.prompt: If `temperature = 31` and `temperature > 30`, which branch runs?
items.explanation: 31 is greater than 30.

items.prompt: A boolean expression always evaluates to `true` or ...
items.explanation: Boolean expressions have exactly two possible outcomes.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Trace loops
assessments.description: Checks whether simple repetition can be simulated.

items.prompt: `count = 0`; a loop increments `count` by 1 three times. What is the final value?
items.explanation: 0 becomes 3 after three iterations.

items.prompt: Which note helps most when tracing a loop?
options: Write down only the first line
Record the counter and important variables for each iteration
items.correctAnswer: Record the counter and important variables for each iteration
items.explanation: That keeps the changing state visible from one round to the next.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Debugging with hypotheses
assessments.description: Checks whether a structured debugging workflow is understood.

items.prompt: Which step comes before fixing a bug?
items.explanation: Before fixing anything, it must be clear where and how the bug appears.

items.prompt: Put the debugging workflow in order.
items.explanation: A clear workflow reduces random action and creates evidence.

assessments.title: Review: Read sequential flow
assessments.description: Short review for the core skill of linear program flow.

items.prompt: Which pattern is fundamental in many programs?
items.explanation: This basic pattern remains visible even in more complex programs.

assessments.title: Review: Track variable state
assessments.description: Short review for state changes.

items.prompt: `points = 10`; then `points = points - 4`. Final value?

assessments.title: Review: Boolean branches
assessments.description: Short review for if/else behavior.

items.prompt: A condition evaluates to `true` or ...
items.explanation: Boolean decisions are binary.

assessments.title: Review: Trace loops
assessments.description: Short review for simple loop simulation.

items.prompt: What is tracing used for in loops?
items.explanation: Tracing reduces complexity to controlled intermediate steps.

assessments.title: Review: Debugging with hypotheses
assessments.description: Short review for reproducible debugging.

items.prompt: What reduces debugging uncertainty most effectively?
options: Forming a hypothesis and testing it directly
items.correctAnswer: Forming a hypothesis and testing it directly
items.explanation: Systematic testing replaces guessing with evidence.

assessments.title: CS1 entry diagnostic
assessments.description: Determines prior knowledge across data types, functions, lists, and basic algorithms.

items.prompt: Which of the following values is a string?
items.explanation: A string is a sequence of characters enclosed in quotes.

items.prompt: What is the result of 10 % 3?
items.explanation: 10 = 3 * 3 + 1, so the remainder is 1.

items.prompt: What does calling a function do?
options: Executes the function body at that point in the program
items.correctAnswer: Executes the function body at that point in the program
items.explanation: A function call transfers execution to the function body.

items.prompt: A function double(n) returns n * 2. What does double(6) return?
List colors = ["red", "green", "blue"]. What is colors[1]?
items.explanation: Index 1 refers to the second element, which is "green".

items.prompt: A loop sums all elements of [2, 4, 6]. What is the final sum?
Which property is required for a set of steps to be an algorithm?
options: It must run in less than one second
Each step must be precise and unambiguous
It must contain at least one loop
items.correctAnswer: Each step must be precise and unambiguous
items.explanation: Precision is essential; ambiguous steps cannot be reliably executed.

items.prompt: Linear search looks for 5 in [8, 3, 5, 1]. Which index is returned?
items.explanation: Index 0 is 8, index 1 is 3, index 2 is 5. The match is at index 2.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Data types
assessments.description: Checks whether the four primitive data types can be identified.

items.prompt: Which type best describes the value 3.14?
items.explanation: 3.14 contains a decimal point, which makes it a float.

items.prompt: Place the type of each value in order: first the type of 42, then the type of true, then the type of "sky".
items.explanation: 42 is an integer, true is a boolean, and "sky" is a string.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Arithmetic operators
assessments.description: Checks whether arithmetic expressions can be evaluated step by step.

items.prompt: Evaluate: 5 + 2 * 3
items.explanation: Multiplication first: 2 * 3 = 6. Then addition: 5 + 6 = 11.

items.prompt: What is 17 % 5?
items.explanation: 17 = 5 * 3 + 2, so the remainder is 2.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Defining and calling functions
assessments.description: Checks whether function definitions and calls are understood.

items.prompt: Which part of a function contains the instructions that run when it is called?
items.explanation: The body holds the instructions that execute when the function is called.

items.prompt: Why would you replace repeated code with a function?
options: To make the program run slower
To eliminate duplication and create a single named reusable unit
Because functions are required by all programs
items.correctAnswer: To eliminate duplication and create a single named reusable unit
items.explanation: Functions remove the need to copy logic, making programs easier to update.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Parameters and return values
assessments.description: Checks whether parameters and return values are understood.

items.prompt: FUNCTION add(a, b): RETURN a + b. What does add(3, 4) return?
items.explanation: a = 3 and b = 4, so a + b = 7.

items.prompt: What is the role of a parameter in a function definition?
options: It stores the final output permanently
It is a named placeholder that receives the argument value when the function is called
It defines how many times the function will run
items.correctAnswer: It is a named placeholder that receives the argument value when the function is called
items.explanation: Parameters are bound to the argument values at the moment of the call.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Lists and indexing
assessments.description: Checks whether list creation and element access are understood.

items.prompt: items = [10, 20, 30, 40]. What is items[2]?
items.explanation: Indexes start at 0. Index 2 holds the third element, which is 30.

items.prompt: A list has 6 elements. What is the index of the last element?
items.explanation: Indexes go from 0 to length minus 1. For 6 elements the last index is 5.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Iterating over lists
assessments.description: Checks whether for-each loops and accumulation are understood.

items.prompt: A loop accumulates the product of [2, 3, 4], starting from 1. What is the final product?
How many times does the loop body execute for the list [5, 10, 15, 20]?
items.explanation: The loop visits each of the 4 elements exactly once.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Algorithm steps
assessments.description: Checks whether the properties of an algorithm and step-based thinking are understood.

items.prompt: Which of the following is NOT a required property of an algorithm?
options: Written in a specific programming language
items.correctAnswer: Written in a specific programming language
items.explanation: Algorithms are language-independent; they describe logic, not syntax.

items.prompt: Put the steps for finding the larger of a and b in the correct order.
options: IF a > b SET larger = a ELSE SET larger = b
correctOrder: IF a > b SET larger = a ELSE SET larger = b
items.explanation: Input comes first, then the decision, then the output.

assessments.title: Lesson check: Linear search
assessments.description: Checks whether linear search can be traced and its behavior understood.

items.prompt: Linear search looks for 4 in [6, 2, 4, 8]. How many comparisons are made before the match is found?
items.explanation: Comparison 1: 6 ≠ 4. Comparison 2: 2 ≠ 4. Comparison 3: 4 = 4. Three comparisons total.

items.prompt: When does linear search perform the most comparisons?
options: When the target is the first element
When the target is the last element or not present
items.correctAnswer: When the target is the last element or not present
items.explanation: In the worst case every element must be examined.

assessments.title: Review: Identify data types
assessments.description: Short review for recognizing primitive data types.

items.prompt: Which type does the value "false" (with quotes) belong to?
items.explanation: Quotes make any sequence of characters a string, regardless of content.

assessments.title: Review: Arithmetic operators
assessments.description: Short review for evaluating arithmetic expressions.

items.prompt: Evaluate: (3 + 5) * 2
items.explanation: Parentheses first: 3 + 5 = 8. Then 8 * 2 = 16.

assessments.title: Review: Define and call functions
assessments.description: Short review for function definitions and calls.

items.prompt: What happens when a function is called?
options: A new variable is automatically created
items.explanation: Calling a function transfers control to the function body.

assessments.title: Review: Parameters and return values
assessments.description: Short review for function parameters and return values.

items.prompt: FUNCTION square(n): RETURN n * n. What does square(7) return?

assessments.title: Review: Create and index lists
assessments.description: Short review for list access by index.

items.prompt: nums = [5, 10, 15]. What is nums[0]?
items.explanation: Index 0 holds the first element, which is 5.

assessments.title: Review: Iterate over lists
assessments.description: Short review for for-each iteration.

items.prompt: A for-each loop over [1, 2, 3, 4, 5] runs the body how many times?
items.explanation: The body runs once per element; there are 5 elements.

assessments.title: Review: Algorithm steps
assessments.description: Short review for algorithm properties.

items.prompt: Which word describes an algorithm that always terminates?
items.explanation: A finite algorithm reaches a stopping point for every valid input.

assessments.title: Review: Linear search
assessments.description: Short review for linear search behavior.

items.prompt: Linear search finds 3 in [7, 3, 9]. What is the index of 3?
items.explanation: Index 0 is 7, index 1 is 3. The match is at index 1.

reviews.title: Review for parameters and return values
